definition: Cherry-picking data clusters to support an idea, or drawing a bullseye around a random cluster of data points after the fact to create the illusion of a pattern. Analogy: Firing a gun at a barn wall and then drawing a target circle around the bullet holes.
example: The company pointed to three clients who doubled their sales using their software, ignoring the thousands of clients whose sales remained flat.

meaning: The ability to attribute mental statesâ€”beliefs, desires, intentionsâ€”to oneself and others.
definition: The cognitive ability to understand that other people have beliefs, desires, and perspectives that are different from one's own.
example: Theory of mind allows a child to understand that a friend may not know what they know.

meaning: Believing that mass media messages have a greater effect on others than on oneself. Analogy: Thinking that advertising campaigns manipulate everyone else, while believing you only buy things out of pure necessity.
definition: Believing that mass media messages have a greater effect on others than on oneself. Analogy: Thinking that advertising campaigns manipulate everyone else, while believing you only buy things out of pure necessity.
example: Violent video games make other teenagers aggressive, but they have absolutely no effect on my own behavior.

meaning: A sequence of connected thoughts or ideas in a person's mind.
definition: A sequence of connected thoughts or ideas in a person's mind.
example: The loud noise outside broke my train of thought, and I forgot what I was saying.

meaning: Going beyond the limits of ordinary experience; surpassing the universe or material existence
definition: Going beyond the limits of ordinary experience; surpassing the universe or material existence
example: Traditional religions portray God as transcendent â€” wholly other, existing outside and above the natural order.

meaning: Applying knowledge or skills acquired in one domain to solve problems or accelerate learning in another domain.
definition: A research problem in machine learning that focuses on storing knowledge gained while solving one problem and applying it to a different but related problem.
example: Transfer learning from her finance background gave her an edge in navigating healthcare economics.

meaning: The belief or theory that the human race can evolve beyond its current limitations through science and technology
definition: The belief or theory that the human race can evolve beyond its current limitations through science and technology
example: Transhumanism envisions a future where genetic engineering and brain-computer interfaces redefine human existence.

meaning: To convert information from one form or language to another
definition: To convert information from one form or language to another
example: Can you translate this scientific diagram into plain language?

meaning: Strong loyalty to one's own social group, tribe, or political faction, often accompanied by hostility toward other groups.
definition: Strong loyalty to one's own social group, tribe, or political faction, often accompanied by hostility toward other groups.
example: Modern political tribalism has made it difficult for different parties to compromise on key national issues.

meaning: Whether a statement is true or false (the basic property of propositions in logic).
definition: The attribute assigned to a proposition in respect of its truth or falsehood.
example: In boolean logic, every variable has a truth value of either true or false.

meaning: Dismissing an opponent's argument by pointing out that their own behavior is inconsistent with their conclusion. Analogy: Rejecting your parent's warning about the dangers of smoking because they used to smoke when they were younger.
definition: Dismissing an argument by pointing out that the person making it is not acting consistently with their own claim.
example: How can you tell me to stop wasting money when you just bought an expensive new television last week?

meaning: A basic belief that is not directly spoken but forms the foundation of an argument.
definition: A basic belief that is not directly spoken but forms the foundation of an argument.
example: The underlying assumption of the project is that internet access will remain cheap.

meaning: A formal fallacy in a categorical syllogism where the middle term is not distributed in either the major or minor premise. Analogy: Knowing all dogs have four legs, and all cats have four legs, and concluding that all dogs are cats.
definition: A formal fallacy in a categorical syllogism where the middle term is not distributed in either the major or minor premise. Analogy: Knowing all dogs have four legs, and all cats have four legs, and concluding that all dogs are cats.
example: All geniuses drink coffee, and my neighbor drinks coffee; therefore, my neighbor is a genius.

meaning: To reveal or become known gradually.
definition: To reveal or become known gradually.
example: As the story unfolded, the meaning of the mysterious word 'harbinger' became apparent.

meaning: The deliberate process of discarding outdated knowledge, habits, or assumptions to make room for new understanding.
definition: The deliberate process of discarding outdated knowledge, habits, or assumptions to make room for new understanding.
example: Unlearning old coding habits was essential before the team could adopt the new architecture.

meaning: The ethical doctrine that the morally right action is the one that produces the greatest good for the greatest number
definition: The doctrine that actions are right if they are useful or for the benefit of a majority.
example: A utilitarian calculation might justify sacrificing one person to save five, regardless of other moral concerns.

meaning: Something that can change or be changed; not fixed.
definition: A quantity that may change within the context of a mathematical problem or experiment.
example: Weather is a variable that can affect outdoor events significantly.

meaning: The truthfulness or accuracy of a statement or claim.
definition: The truthfulness or accuracy of a statement or claim.
example: We questioned the veracity of his story because he had lied in the past.

meaning: To make sure or demonstrate that something is true, accurate, or justified.
definition: To make sure that something is true, accurate, or justified (replaces the conversational 'double check').
example: After guessing the word's meaning from context, she verified it with a dictionary.

meaning: A particular attitude or way of considering a matter; a point of view.
definition: A particular attitude or way of considering a matter; a point of view.
example: The essay must present and evaluate multiple viewpoints on the topic.

meaning: An approach to ethics that emphasizes the character and virtues of the moral agent rather than rules or consequences
definition: An approach to ethics that emphasizes an individual's character as the key element of ethical thinking.
example: Aristotle's virtue ethics focuses on cultivating traits like courage and justice to live a flourishing life.

meaning: The formation of a mental image of something.
definition: The formation of a mental image of something.
example: Visualization of a 'tranquil' scene helped him connect the word to its meaning of peacefulness.

meaning: A reason or explanation that connects your evidence to your main claim.
definition: A reason or explanation that connects your evidence to your main claim.
example: If you claim it will rain, the dark clouds in the sky are your warrant.

meaning: A comprehensive world view; a particular philosophy or conception of the world held by an individual or group
definition: A comprehensive world view; a particular philosophy or conception of the world held by an individual or group
example: His scientific Weltanschauung made it impossible for him to accept explanations rooted in supernatural causation.

meaning: A mental web of related words and concepts connected to a central word.
definition: A mental web of related words and concepts connected to a central word.
example: Building a word network around 'courage' deepened her understanding of related vocabulary.

meaning: A pre-existing mental framework related to a concept that helps new vocabulary be understood and remembered within context.
definition: A pre-existing mental framework related to a concept that helps new vocabulary be understood and remembered within context.
example: Your word schema for 'hospital' already includes doctors, nurses, beds, and medicine, making new hospital vocabulary easier to learn.

meaning: The system that temporarily holds and manipulates information during cognitive tasks.
definition: The system that temporarily holds and manipulates information during cognitive tasks.
example: Solving a math problem in your head relies heavily on working memory.

meaning: The tendency to remember interrupted or uncompleted tasks better than completed ones. Analogy: Remembering the questions you got wrong or didn't finish on an exam much more clearly than the ones you answered correctly.
definition: The tendency to remember interrupted or uncompleted tasks better than completed ones. Analogy: Remembering the questions you got wrong or didn't finish on an exam much more clearly than the ones you answered correctly.
example: I cannot sleep because I keep thinking about the half-finished report sitting on my office desk.
